problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem A (continuous): given that a vaccine/cure for the 2014-15 West Africa Ebola outbreak might be developed in limited initial supply, build a model of (a) how the outbreak spreads and (b) the optimal strategy for delivering/distributing the medicine to minimize loss of life. Team 32722 ("Turning the Tables") restricted scope to the three actively-infected countries (Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone) over a 6-month horizon and built a custom agent-based, multi-scale stochastic epidemic + logistics simulation to test distribution tactics. Their headline (and honest) conclusion: the data is too poor for quantitative prediction, so they deliver QUALITATIVE distribution rules instead — a notable reframing of the task.
models_used: Multi-layer (individual / city-region / nation) metapopulation epidemic model — agent-based, GLEAM-inspired but custom-built

Stochastic state-based compartmental model at the individual level: an extended SIR with Ebola-specific compartments S, Latent, Early-stage infectious (Ie), Late-stage infectious (Il), Hospitalized (H), Cured (C), Unburied corpse (U), Buried (B), Vaccinated — encoded as a directed transition graph (Fig. 4)

Monte-Carlo simulation: each agent draws a Mersenne-Twister random number per timestep and transitions per Table-1 probabilities; interaction/infection probability computed as fraction of infectious-type individuals in region

A modified gravity model for human mobility (their own derivation, Eqs. 3-4), extending the standard gravity model T_ij = P_i^α P_j^β / r_ij^γ; rejected the radiation model because discrete regions can't yield the s_ij density term
A time-varying 'confidence factor' φ_i(t) = exp[((B+D) - (I+U))/P] injected into the mobility model so travel responds dynamically to local beds, doctors, infected, and unburied corpses
Hub-and-spoke supply/logistics distribution model with designated hub cities, percentage-of-available shipping rule, and tunable 'travel rules' (loss/spoilage/frequency)
Companion deterministic ODE compartmental model (Appendix B, Eqs. B.1-B.8) presented as the continuum limit / rigorous formalization of the same state graph

Sensitivity analysis as a first-class method: one-at-a-time parameter sweeps over initial infected, hospital beds, population scale, medicine efficacy, vaccine quantity/frequency, movement factor k, and per-region parameter sets
key_techniques: Multi-scale modular decomposition (individual -> city -> nation) so the same engine scales from a 4-city 'toy model' up to a real geographic network — explicitly separating intra-region dynamics from inter-region coupling

Parameter derivation from real epidemiological literature (Rivers et al. PLOS Currents) rather than guessing: e.g. γ_w1 = 1/incubation period, γ_dh = mortality × 1/(time hospitalization->death), γ_f = 1/(2.01-day funeral), hospitalization prob = 0.197 reported-and-hospitalized × 1/3.24 days — every Table-1 value traced to a real quantity

Modeling Ebola-SPECIFIC mechanics other teams miss: distinct early vs late infectiousness (β_c1 vs β_c2), infection from unburied corpses during traditional funerals (β_f), hospital-bed capacity as a hard, depletable resource that quarantines and limits throughput

Modeling the cure's mechanism honestly as a hospital-throughput multiplier on recovery rate γ_R — surfacing the non-obvious insight that a faster cure frees beds and raises effective hospital capacity (a 'one-two punch')
Toy-model-first methodology: build qualitative understanding on small 4-city systems before attempting full-region simulation; validating each layer in isolation

Self-validation against external ground truth: the model's emergent self-limiting (flare-out) behavior matched WHO situation reports and historical Ebola outbreaks, used as evidence of behavioral fidelity
Reframing the deliverable: explicitly arguing that >20 coupled, poorly-known parameters make quantitative prediction impossible, then pivoting to defensible qualitative distribution laws
Engineering for performance/scale: Python with class abstractions (Individual/Region/Connection), PyPy interpreter, NumPy/Matplotlib pipeline — acknowledging and working around computational limits
why_it_won: "It out-modeled the field on domain specificity and intellectual honesty simultaneously. Most teams would slap an SIR/SEIR ODE on Ebola; this team built a genuinely multi-scale, stochastic, agent-based metapopulation simulator that captured the features that actually drive Ebola — two-stage infectiousness, corpse/funeral transmission, finite quarantining hospital beds, and dynamic fear-driven migration — and then derived nearly every parameter from real WHO/PLOS data with a traceable formula. Crucially, judges reward teams that interrogate their own model: this paper ran a broad, well-organized sensitivity analysis, discovered an unsettling population-scale dependence, listed 15 explicit assumptions with honest critiques (even admitting the 'vaccine sink' bug their distribution rule caused), and concluded that quantitative prediction was NOT defensible — converting that limitation into clean qualitative recommendations (vaccinate ahead of the front to outpace infection; send cures to at-capacity facilities; never stop building hospitals). The actionable, mechanism-grounded strategy plus the GLEAM-style architecture and the differential-equation appendix giving mathematical rigor showed both engineering ambition and modeling maturity — exactly the combination judges look for."
section_highlights: "Abstract states the multi-layer thesis AND the honest qualitative-only conclusion up front — judges know the punchline immediately. Section 1 gives strong context, a literature lineage (Bernoulli -> Kermack-McKendrick SIR -> agent-based GLEAM) that motivates the chosen method, and a candid account of why they couldn't use GLEAM itself (closed-source black box). Section 2 layers the model top-down with a clear compartment directed graph (Fig. 4) and a fully-sourced parameter table (Table 1). Section 3 is the centerpiece: systematic, captioned sensitivity sweeps each tied back to a real-world worry (e.g. under-reporting -> vary initial infected; bed construction -> vary bed count). 'Consequences of Complexity' (2.6) pre-empts the quantitative-prediction critique. The conclusion's 'The Art of Eradicating Ebola' distills crisp, prioritized guidance, and the memorable 'Zeroeth Law' (mind the system's delicacy) gives the strategy a voice. Appendix A's 15 numbered assumptions and Appendix B's ODE system add rigor and self-awareness. The closing fictional 'WHAMO Bulletin' memo is a creative, audience-appropriate delivery of recommendations (with invented drug names Curzimall/Preventatine) that frames the team as 'three interns given 96 hours' — confident and on-brand for the problem prompt."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Quantitatively non-predictive by the team's own admission: >20 coupled parameters from unreliable data means no calibrated forecast — they could only assert qualitative trends and an order-of-magnitude 'doses scale with infected count'

Unexplained and worrying population-scale sensitivity: results changed when population/hospitals were scaled down 10x (forced by compute limits), because hospital-seeking probability and length-of-stay didn't scale with population — a known but unresolved bug that undermines the multi-city runs
Distribution rule had a real defect: the percentage-of-available shipping law created 'vaccine sink' cities that hoarded supply and broke large-scale simulations (Assumption 12)
Gravity-mobility bias: urban nodes draw population but don't return it, biasing every urban-vs-rural distribution conclusion (they flag this honestly but it taints Fig. 9/10 interpretation)
No averaging over stochastic runs (Assumption 13) — single realizations of a Monte-Carlo model presented as results, with visible run-to-run variation, so reported numbers lack confidence intervals

Strong idealizations: 100%-effective single-dose vaccine, 0% effective on already-infected, cure only works in hospitals and not modeled as less effective for late-stage patients (acknowledged oversight), indefinite drug shelf life, constant per-country parameters, closed-system (no external import/export)
Guinea parameters borrowed wholesale from Liberia due to missing data; mobility constants (α,β,γ) fit only to Guinea census microdata then reused for Liberia/Sierra Leone without validation
The full real-geography model they designed for was never actually implemented — results rest on toy 4-city systems and single-region tests, not the intended deliverable
Chart data as transcribed is coarse/approximate (many '~' values), and some sensitivity conclusions ('roughly within ten percent') are eyeballed rather than quantified
reusable_patterns: Multi-scale metapopulation template: individuals as state machines inside region-nodes inside a region-graph, with intra-region (compartment) rules decoupled from inter-region (mobility/logistics) rules — reusable for any spatial-spread problem (disease, rumor, supply, fire)
Parameter-provenance table: a Table-1-style artifact where every coefficient has a value, a source citation, and a one-line derivation formula — a fast credibility multiplier for any modeling paper
Build-your-own when the off-the-shelf tool is a black box, but cite and draw inspiration from it (GLEAM) — turns a tooling limitation into a methodological strength
Toy-model-first then scale: validate each layer on tiny synthetic systems, treat toy and real data identically in code so scaling is trivial, get qualitative insight before expensive full runs

Sensitivity analysis structured around real-world uncertainties: pick which parameters to sweep based on what data is actually unreliable (under-reporting, bed counts, mobility), not arbitrarily — and let a surprising sensitivity become a reported finding
Honest-limitation pivot: when the data can't support quantitative claims, explicitly say so and downgrade to qualitative rules — judges reward this over false precision
Numbered-assumptions appendix where each assumption includes its justification AND its consequence/critique — demonstrates self-awareness and pre-empts reviewer objections
Continuum-limit appendix: provide the deterministic ODE system that your stochastic agent model approximates, giving mathematical rigor alongside simulation

Dynamic behavioral coupling via an exp() 'confidence/utility' factor that feeds local state (beds, deaths, corpses) back into agent decisions (here, migration) — a clean way to make agents react to the simulation state
Audience-tailored deliverable: close with a short in-character memo/bulletin to the decision-maker that states prioritized, actionable recommendations separate from the technical body
